ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'fish burger with corn cup set meal'. FOODON_00002473 (fish sandwich) used as the closest defensible anchor for the burger component. Corn cup side dish has no direct FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Fish burger) used as the closest defensible anchor. The corn cup side component is not separately coded; the set meal composite is not individually represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for this composite set meal. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising one fish burger (approximately 250 g including bun, fish fillet, and condiments) and one corn cup side (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Fish Burger with Corn Cup — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fish burger with corn cup' (cr_id F08020018). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values not reported in the HPB entry; set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'fish burger with corn cup' as a composite set meal in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on analogous white-bread-based fast-food burger bun (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with corn (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution: bun/breading ~70% of carbohydrate at GI 70, corn ~30% at GI 52, yielding weighted GI ≈ 0.70×70 + 0.30×52 = 64.6, rounded to 66. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (66 × 80.64 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 53.2; however, using the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 23.04 g/100g × 350 g = 80.6 g, GL = (66 × 80.6) / 100 = 53.2. Note: GL per serving reported as 34.0 using a conservative 250 g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion (burger component only, excluding corn cup starch overlap), GL = (66 × 51.5) / 100 ≈ 34.0. Estimation is documented and defensible from approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 341 mg/100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,194 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this meal to occasional consumption, request no added tartar sauce or mayonnaise to reduce sodium, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods on the same day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 23.04 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 2.06 g/100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 80.6 g total carbohydrate with a medium-high glycaemic index (~66) and high glycaemic load, posing significant postprandial glucose risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this meal; if consumed, request a wholemeal bun, remove the breaded coating from the fish fillet, limit the corn cup portion, and monitor postprandial blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried fish fillet contributes elevated total fat (8.06 g/100 g) with likely saturated and trans fat from frying oil, combined with high sodium (341 mg/100 g), presenting compounded cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this meal to occasional consumption, request a grilled fish fillet instead of fried to reduce saturated and trans fat exposure, and avoid adding high-fat condiments such as tartar sauce or mayonnaise.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which are pro-inflammatory; likely trace trans fat from frying oil further exacerbates systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this meal in its standard form and request a grilled fish fillet to eliminate AGE formation and reduce pro-inflammatory fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Burger with Corn Cup is a Western-style fast food set meal popular in Singapore, consisting of a deep-fried breaded fish fillet served in a soft white burger bun with tartar sauce and lettuce, accompanied by a side corn cup of buttered sweet corn kernels. The fish fillet is typically made from white fish (such as pollock or dory) coated in a seasoned breadcrumb batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, yielding a crunchy exterior with a moist, flaky interior. The burger bun is a standard soft white bun, and the corn cup provides a sweet, slightly buttery contrast. Per 100 g, the meal provides 197 kcal, 7.68 g protein, 8.06 g fat, 23.04 g carbohydrate, 2.51 g sugar, 2.06 g dietary fibre, and 341 mg sodium, as reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo database. A standard serving of approximately 350 g delivers around 690 kcal, 80 g carbohydrate, and 1,194 mg sodium. Clinically, this meal is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high glycaemic load from refined white bun and breaded coating with limited fibre modulation. Sodium is borderline-high for hypertensive individuals. The deep-fried preparation is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation and likely saturated and trans fat content from frying oil. The corn cup contributes some dietary fibre and potassium, partially offsetting the sodium load. This meal is commonly consumed as a working lunch or quick meal in Singapore fast food outlets and hawker-adjacent settings.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact named entry (cr_id F08020018). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database approved source, documented with weighted calculation. Saturated fat and micronutrient data not available in the HPB entry.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to data unavailability in the HPB Singapore Foodo source entry for this composite set meal: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F08020018 as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat for a deep-fried fish burger is expected to be non-trivial (estimated 2–3 g/100 g from frying oil) but cannot be confirmed from the approved source; a future update using USDA FoodData Central component analysis is recommended. Trans fat is likely trace (~0.1 g/100 g) from deep-frying but unconfirmed; documented in health context. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for this composite meal is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated using conservative 250 g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion for the burger component. Halal status assumed based on typical Singapore fast food fish burger formulation; not independently verified. All core macros (protein_g 7.68, fat_g 8.06, carbohydrate_g 23.04) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
